message.content: i notice that generally things like editing etc are rather slow, e.g. adding a wall. please analyze deeply what is going on here. is there some subsantial issue?

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Analyze slow editing and wall addition performance

content.text: Let me do a deep analysis of the performance characteristics in the codebase, focusing on editing operations like adding walls.

input.description: Deep performance analysis of editing operations
input.prompt:
I need a thorough performance analysis of a BIM (Building Information Modeling) web application. The user reports that editing operations like "adding a wall" are slow. Please do a deep investigation.

Key areas to investigate:
1. State management patterns - how state updates propagate (look for excessive re-renders, large state trees being mutated)
2. The wall tool / any wall-related code - how adding a wall works end-to-end
3. React rendering patterns - useMemo, useCallback, component tree, any expensive renders
4. Data flow architecture - how commands/actions flow through the system
5. Any synchronous expensive operations (geometry computation, 3D rendering)
6. Event handlers and their debouncing/throttling
7. Any obvious N² or repeated work patterns

The repo is at /Users/jhoetter/repos/bim-ai. Please explore:
- packages/web/src/ (React frontend)
- packages/web/src/tools/ (tool system including wall tool)
- packages/web/src/workspace/ (workspace components)
- Any state management files (zustand, redux, context, etc.)
- Any geometry/computation files
- The Viewport.tsx and related rendering

Look for:
- Large components that re-render on every state change
- State updates that trigger whole-tree re-renders
- Expensive computations not memoized
- Synchronous operations blocking the main thread
- Any patterns that would make "add wall" specifically slow

Please be thorough and report actual file paths and line numbers for issues found. Search breadth: very thorough.

content.content:

content.text:
The analysis is confirmed. This is a **fundamental architectural problem** — here's what's happening every time you add a wall:

---

## Root Cause: Full Scene Nuke-and-Rebuild on Every State Change

**`Viewport.tsx:1806–2062`** — there's a single `useEffect` that fires whenever `elementsById` changes (which happens on every edit). The very first thing it does:

```ts
// line 1811
while (root.children.length) root.remove(root.children[0]!);
```

It **destroys the entire 3D scene**, then rebuilds every single element from scratch — floors, walls, doors, windows, stairs, rooms, roofs, railings, sites — with **8 separate passes** over `Object.values(elementsById)`. This is O(N) work per every edit.

---

## The dependency array (lines 2054–2062) makes it worse

```ts
}, [
  elementsById,    // ← fires on every wall add
  selectedId,      // ← fires on every click/selection
  viewerCategoryHidden,
  viewerClipElevMm,
  viewerClipFloorElevMm,
  sectionBoxActive,
  theme,
]);
```

Even **selecting an element** triggers a full scene rebuild because `selectedId` is in the deps. With 50 elements that's manageable; with 200+ it becomes visibly sluggish.

---

## Secondary issue: Synchronous CSG operations

`makeWallWithOpenings` (line 778) runs synchronous boolean subtractions via `three-bvh-csg` for every wall that has hosted doors/windows. Each `evaluator.evaluate(wallBrush, cutter, SUBTRACTION)` blocks the main thread. As you accumulate more walls-with-openings, this cost grows linearly.

---

## Severity breakdown

| Issue | Impact |
|---|---|
| Nuke + rebuild full scene on `elementsById` change | **Critical** — O(N) per any edit |
| Nuke + rebuild on `selectedId` change | **High** — every click re-renders everything |
| Synchronous CSG per hosted opening | **High** — grows with wall count |
| 8 separate `Object.values()` passes | **Medium** — easy to consolidate |
| `buildSkyEnvMap` called every rebuild (line 2029) | **Medium** — expensive env map re-bake |

---

## The fix strategy

The right approach is **incremental scene management**: instead of rebuilding everything, maintain a `Map<elementId, THREE.Object3D>` and only add/update/remove the meshes that actually changed between `elementsById` snapshots. This turns O(N) rebuild into O(delta) per edit.

For `selectedId` specifically: no geometry rebuild is needed at all — just swap the material color on the affected mesh(es) in-place, which is a single material update.

Want me to implement the incremental scene manager? It's the highest-leverage change by far.
